metadata.purpose: Gyroporus, Chalciporus, and associated smaller bolete groups.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.culinary_uses: Edible reputation exists, but this guide treats it as a cautious ID-first species because blue-staining boletes need care.

mushroom_profile.summary: Straw-colored brittle bolete with dramatic blue staining and a yellow spore print; usually in sandy hardwood-edge habitats.
research_notes: Straw-yellow cap and stem, brittle/chalky texture, strong blue staining when cut or bruised, and yellow spore print separate it from most boletes.

records.edibility_detail: Do not treat every blue-staining bolete as this species; confirm straw-yellow color, brittle or hollowing stem, immediate blue staining, sandy/disturbed habitat, and yellow spore print.
records.mushroom_card_note: Straw-colored brittle bolete with dramatic blue staining and a yellow spore print; usually in sandy hardwood-edge habitats.
records.field_identification: Straw-yellow cap and stem, brittle/chalky texture, strong blue staining when cut or bruised, and yellow spore print separate it from most boletes.

records.food_value: Not a food target in this guide
records.risk_reason: Blue staining makes beginners overconfident; this record should teach the straw-yellow, brittle/hollowing, immediately blue-staining concept rather than invite casual eating.
records.quality_notes: Included for recognition, comparison, or caution rather than table value.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
records.hard_rule: Do not treat every blue-staining bolete as this species; confirm straw-yellow color, brittle or hollowing stem, immediate blue staining, sandy/disturbed habitat, and yellow spore print.
records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.
records.image_rights_notes: v4.3.214 retained only exact-file-page verified visible images; unresolved and stale exposures were removed from all visible image fields.

records.culinary_uses: Some Gyroporus chestnut boletes are edible, but this guide treats the borealis/smithii record conservatively until the species split is better confirmed regionally.
records.notes: Chestnut-type Gyroporus: brittle hollow stem, chestnut-brown cap, and yellowish spore-print group; treat species boundary as conservative.

mushroom_profile.summary: Chestnut-bolete complex covering the broad North American Gyroporus castaneus concept, including proposed G. borealis and G. smithii segregates that are not safely separated in the field.
research_notes: Species boundary remains conservative; do not promote as food without stronger regional species-level confidence.
Gyroporus records should be checked for brittle/hollow stem tissue, dry cap, lack of strong reticulation, and association with hardwoods or mixed woods.

records.edibility_detail: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.
records.mushroom_card_note: Chestnut-type Gyroporus: brittle hollow stem, chestnut-brown cap, and yellowish spore-print group; treat species boundary as conservative.
records.field_identification: Use chestnut-brown cap and stem, white to pale yellow pores, brittle hollowing stem, and pale-yellow print only for broad complex-level identification. Narrow borealis/smithii assignments require specialist microscopic and multilocus work.
records.food_value: Not a food target in this guide
records.risk_reason: Current confidence, food value, or field separation is not strong enough for a food recommendation. Keep searchable for comparison and field ID.
records.quality_notes: Included for recognition, comparison, or caution rather than table value.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
records.hard_rule: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.

images_structured.scope_note: Representative for Gyroporus borealis within the app’s Gyroporus borealis/smithii conservative split record; not generic Gyroporus castaneus.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Genus Gyroporus
use_links.notes: Genus-level support for brittle/hollow-stemmed Gyroporus recognition; species split remains conservative in this app record.

records.taxonomy_note: Retained as a broad Gyroporus castaneus complex. The proposed G. borealis and G. smithii segregates are not treated as independently field-identifiable app species without stronger multilocus support.
records.source_note: v4.3.211 adds a defensible complex-level pale-yellow print while keeping review open for narrow segregate taxonomy.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food target in this guide; use as an identification/comparison record until the species is better documented locally.

mushroom_profile.summary: Red to purplish Gyroporus-type bolete with brittle flesh and a hollowing stem; included mainly for comparison.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September; treated conservatively with other eastern Gyroporus/boletes until species-level Upper Michigan timing is better documented.
research_notes: Still partial: genus-level source only; species-level source and image-rights metadata modernization are still needed for full cleanup.

records.mushroom_card_note: Red to purplish Gyroporus-type bolete with brittle flesh and a hollowing stem; included as an ID/comparison species rather than a prime target.
records.field_identification: Check for Gyroporus traits: brittle/chalky flesh, stem that may become hollow, dry cap, pores rather than gills, and red to purplish tones. Compare against other Gyroporus and small red/brown boletes.
records.edibility_detail: Treat conservatively. Gyroporus species boundaries and local relevance need confirmation before this belongs in any edible target list.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Gyroporus genus overview
use_links.notes: Genus-level support only; species-level source and image-rights modernization remain blockers for full cleanup.

records.image_coverage_note: No visible image remains after exact file-page rights closeout; the record remains available without imagery.
records.image_rights_notes: v4.3.214 removed unresolved or scope-mismatched images from all visible image fields. Do not restore without exact file-page rights and appropriate taxon scope.

records.culinary_uses: ID/comparison species only in this guide; not presented as a food mushroom.

mushroom_profile.summary: Peppery reddish-pored bolete like C. piperatus, but with blue bruising and an olive spore print; originally described from Michigan.
research_notes: Small drab pinkish-tan to reddish-brown bolete with reddish pores, yellow basal mycelium, and peppery/bitter taste; unlike C. piperatus, it can bruise blue and has an olive spore print.

records.edibility_detail: Use as an ID/comparison species only in this guide. The peppery/bitter taste clue should be nibble-and-spit only, and only after the mushroom is already believed safe to taste.
records.mushroom_card_note: Peppery reddish-pored bolete like C. piperatus, but with blue bruising and an olive spore print; originally described from Michigan.
records.field_identification: Small drab pinkish-tan to reddish-brown bolete with reddish pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a peppery/bitter taste. Unlike C. piperatus, it can bruise blue and has an olive spore print.
records.edibility_notes: Use as an ID/comparison species only in this guide. The peppery/bitter taste clue should be nibble-and-spit only, and only after the mushroom is already believed safe to taste.

edible_use.notes: Use as an ID/comparison species only in this guide. The peppery/bitter taste clue should be nibble-and-spit only, and only after the mushroom is already believed safe to taste.

records.source_note: Source review cleared in v4.3.159-visible-debt-cleanup1 using MushroomExpert species-level ID support; record remains ID/comparison-only and food-conservative because the source does not support food promotion. v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Chalciporus piperatoides
use_links.notes: Supports species-level ID traits and Michigan relevance; no food-promotion support.

records.culinary_uses: Peppery seasoning curiosity / ID clue only; not a normal food target.

mushroom_profile.summary: Classic peppery bolete: dull reddish-brown cap, reddish pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a sharp peppery taste.
research_notes: Look for a small drab reddish-brown to pinkish-tan bolete with brownish-red pores that darken but do not blue, yellow basal mycelium, and a very peppery taste. Usually under conifers but not exclusively.

records.edibility_detail: Treat the peppery taste as an ID clue, not a casual snack test. Nibble-and-spit only after the mushroom is already believed safe to taste; do not present this as a normal edible target.
records.mushroom_card_note: Classic peppery bolete: dull reddish-brown cap, reddish pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a sharp peppery taste.
records.field_identification: Look for a small drab reddish-brown to pinkish-tan bolete with brownish-red pores that darken but do not blue, yellow basal mycelium, and a very peppery taste. Usually under conifers but not exclusively.
records.edibility_notes: Treat the peppery taste as an ID clue, not a casual snack test. Nibble-and-spit only after the mushroom is already believed safe to taste; do not present this as a normal edible target.

records.source_note: Unverified Chalciporus piperatus.jpeg image dropped in v4.3.155; remaining images have verified rights metadata. Food-role framing changed away from normal food in v4.3.159-visible-debt-cleanup1; niche peppery-use wording retained as caution/ID context. v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

edible_use.notes: ID/comparison and peppery-seasoning curiosity only; not presented as a normal food target in this guide.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Chalciporus piperatus
use_links.notes: Supports field ID traits; app keeps taste as an ID clue rather than a food recommendation.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as a food target in this guide; retained as a specialist comparison among small red-pored boletes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Small red-pored Chalciporus with yellow flesh and pinkish-red pores; type material was collected in Michigan, but use a conservative ID standard.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July to September in reported North American material.
research_notes: Small yellow to red-pink cap, red-pink pores fading yellow-orange/brown, yellow flesh that does not blue, and yellow-based pinkish stem.
Food role downgraded pending stronger non-Wikipedia source support.

records.edibility_detail: Specialist-ID comparison only. Do not use this app record as a food recommendation unless a stronger edible-use source and local confidence are added later.
records.mushroom_card_note: Small red-pored Chalciporus with yellow flesh and pinkish-red pores; type material was collected in Michigan, but use a conservative ID standard.
records.field_identification: Look for a small yellow to red-pink cap, red-pink pores fading yellow-orange/brown, yellow flesh that does not blue, and a yellow-based pinkish stem. Best treated as a specialist Chalciporus ID.
records.edibility_notes: Treat as specialist-ID only. Confirm the small yellow to red-pink cap, red-pink pores fading orange-brown, yellow flesh that does not blue, and yellow-based pinkish stem;

edible_use.notes: Safety downgraded pending stronger non-Wikipedia source support.

records.image_rights_notes: Single exact-species Wikimedia Commons / Mushroom Observer image rights normalized in v4.3.150; record remains review-needed because non-Wikipedia species source support is still weak.
records.source_note: Image rights cleaned, but species/source review remains open; do not clear review without stronger non-Wikipedia support. v4.3.211 records the nomenclatural blocker and deliberately leaves spore and host claims unchanged.
records.taxonomy_note: Original Michigan taxon retained as an unresolved specialist comparison pending direct protologue and modern nomenclatural reconciliation.

records.culinary_uses: Comparison species rather than a main food mushroom.

mushroom_profile.summary: Small purple-red/red-pored bolete; useful as a comparison species among peppery/red-pored Chalciporus rather than a major edible.
research_notes: Small convex cap often purple-red to reddish brown, red to pinkish pore surface, yellow flesh that generally does not blue, and a bare stem. Compare with C. pseudorubinellus and C. piperatus.

records.edibility_detail: Use caution with small red-pored boletes. Confirm red to pinkish pores, yellow flesh that generally does not blue, and separation from C. pseudorubinellus and C. piperatus.
records.mushroom_card_note: Small purple-red/red-pored bolete; useful as a comparison species among peppery/red-pored Chalciporus rather than a major edible.
records.field_identification: Small convex cap often purple-red to reddish brown, red to pinkish pore surface, yellow flesh that generally does not blue, and a bare stem. Compare with C. pseudorubinellus and C. piperatus.
records.edibility_notes: Use caution with small red-pored boletes. Confirm red to pinkish pores, yellow flesh that generally does not blue, and separation from C. pseudorubinellus and C. piperatus.

records.image_rights_notes: Two Chalciporus rubinellus images restored in v4.3.159-visible-debt-cleanup1 with exact Wikimedia Commons / Mushroom Observer CC BY-SA 3.0 metadata. Earlier v4.3.158 image clearing is superseded by this verification pass.
records.source_note: Verified Chalciporus rubinellus images restored in v4.3.159-visible-debt-cleanup1 after exact Commons file-page rights verification. Species/source review remains open because strong non-Wikipedia profile support still needs supervisor verification; keep as ID/comparison only. v4.3.211 records that likely conifer ecology and a brown print remain uninstalled until the exact Baroni or MycoQuébec species passage is directly verified.

use_links.label: NAMA: Mushrooms to Dye For
use_links.notes: Mentions Chalciporus rubinellus in dye context only; does not support edible use or field ID.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as food; more useful for recognizing the bright yellow-veiled bolete form.

mushroom_profile.summary: Bright yellow-veiled bolete when young, often with reddish/orange cap tones and blue-green bruising on pores.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; primarily with conifers, especially pine, hemlock, and spruce.

records.mushroom_card_note: Bright yellow-veiled bolete when young, often with reddish/orange cap tones and blue-green bruising on pores.
records.field_identification: Young mushrooms have a powdery bright yellow veil over the pore surface and stem; older mushrooms show yellow veil remnants, reddish to orange cap colors, yellowish pores, and greenish-blue bruising.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid as a food target. Confirm the powdery yellow veil in young specimens, yellowish pores, reddish/orange cap tones, and greenish-blue bruising.

use_links.label: Wikimedia Commons: Pulveroboletus ravenelii

records.edibility_notes: Avoid as a food target. Confirm the powdery yellow veil in young specimens, yellowish pores, reddish/orange cap tones, and greenish-blue bruising.
